title_en: Marriage and Family Law in Nihayat al-Muhtaj
title_ar: Marriage and Family Law in Nihayat al-Muhtaj
content_en:
Ar-Ramli's treatment of nikah and family law in Nihayat al-Muhtaj combines the Shafi'i school's rigorous legal formalism with practical wisdom born from his role as the leading mufti of Egypt. His positions in this chapter are widely followed in Shafi'i communities across the Arab world and parts of Southeast Asia.

The Shafi'i requirement of the wali (guardian) as a condition of a valid marriage contract is confirmed and elaborated by ar-Ramli. A marriage without the wali is void ab initio (batil), not merely defective — it has no legal effect. The only exception is the ruler or judge acting as wali of last resort for a woman who has no other guardian or whose guardian refuses without just cause (adhal). Ar-Ramli explains the order of guardianship in detail: father, paternal grandfather, full brother, half-brother (paternal), paternal uncle, son of paternal uncle, and so forth through the agnatic line of inheritance.

Ar-Ramli addresses the case of a virgin who has never been married: in the Shafi'i school, the father and paternal grandfather have the power of ijbar (compulsory marriage) over a virgin daughter, meaning they may contract her marriage without her explicit consent — though most scholars add the condition that it must be to a suitable match (kuf') and without apparent harm. This is a distinctive and historically sensitive Shafi'i position. Ar-Ramli explains it as derived from hadiths indicating that the father's right over his virgin daughter's marriage is stronger than her own, while also noting that the Prophet said a virgin's silence constitutes consent when the marriage is proposed to her.

On the kafaah (suitability) of the husband, ar-Ramli specifies the Shafi'i criteria: lineage (nasab), freedom (not being a slave), religion (not a known sinner), profession (not degrading), and financial means. A mismatch in kafaah does not void the marriage but gives the guardian the right to apply to a judge to have it annulled before consummation.

The mahr (dower) section in Nihayat al-Muhtaj covers both the prompt mahr (mu'ajjal) and the deferred mahr (mu'ajjal — paid at divorce or death). Ar-Ramli explains that if no mahr was specified and the marriage is valid, the wife receives the mahr al-mithl (customary equivalent). If the marriage is consummated, the full mahr is due; if it is not consummated and the husband divorces, half the mahr is due (Quran, al-Baqarah 2:237).

Ar-Ramli's discussion of talaq (divorce) covers its conditions, forms, and effects with the precision that characterizes this school's family law. A binding (munajjaz) talaq is effective immediately when pronounced. A conditional talaq (mu'allaq) is effective when the specified condition occurs. Revocable divorce (raj'i) — the first or second talaq where no compensation was exchanged — allows the husband to take the wife back during the iddah without a new contract. After three talaqs, the marriage is finally dissolved and the couple cannot remarry until the woman has completed a valid marriage to another man.

The iddah (waiting period) after divorce is three menstrual cycles for a woman who menstruates, three months for a post-menopausal woman, and full-term pregnancy for a pregnant woman. During the iddah, the divorced wife retains the right to maintenance and housing from her former husband — a right ar-Ramli explains is established by the Quran (at-Talaq 65:6) and the Sunnah. This maintenance obligation is one of the practical legal protections that classical Islamic law built into the divorce process to prevent immediate destitution of divorced women.
title_id: Pernikahan dan Hukum Keluarga dalam Nihayat al-Muhtaj
content_id:
Pembahasan ar-Ramli tentang nikah dan hukum keluarga dalam Nihayat al-Muhtaj memadukan formalisme hukum ketat mazhab Syafi'i dengan kebijaksanaan praktis yang lahir dari perannya sebagai mufti utama Mesir. Posisi-posisinya dalam bab ini banyak diikuti di komunitas Syafi'i di dunia Arab dan sebagian Asia Tenggara.

Persyaratan wali (wali nikah) mazhab Syafi'i sebagai syarat sahnya akad nikah dikonfirmasi dan diuraikan secara luas oleh ar-Ramli. Pernikahan tanpa wali adalah batal sejak semula (batil), bukan hanya cacat — tidak memiliki akibat hukum apa pun. Satu-satunya pengecualian adalah penguasa atau hakim yang bertindak sebagai wali terakhir bagi perempuan yang tidak memiliki wali lain atau yang walinya menolak tanpa alasan yang sah (adhal). Ar-Ramli menjelaskan urutan perwalian secara terperinci: ayah, kakek dari pihak ayah, saudara laki-laki kandung, saudara laki-laki seayah, paman dari pihak ayah, anak paman dari pihak ayah, dan seterusnya melalui jalur waris dari pihak ayah.

Ar-Ramli membahas kasus perempuan yang masih perawan dan belum pernah menikah: dalam mazhab Syafi'i, ayah dan kakek dari pihak ayah memiliki kekuasaan ijbar (menikahkan secara paksa) atas anak perempuan yang masih perawan, yang berarti mereka dapat mengakadkan pernikahannya tanpa persetujuan eksplisitnya — meskipun sebagian besar ulama menambahkan syarat bahwa hal itu harus dilakukan dengan pasangan yang setara (kuf') dan tanpa mudarat yang nyata. Ar-Ramli menjelaskannya sebagai berasal dari hadis-hadis yang menunjukkan bahwa hak ayah atas pernikahan anak perempuannya yang masih perawan lebih kuat dari haknya sendiri.

Tentang kafaah (kesepadanan) suami, ar-Ramli merinci kriteria Syafi'i: nasab (keturunan), kemerdekaan (bukan budak), agama, dan moralitas — semua dipertimbangkan dalam menentukan kesepadanan yang layak untuk sebuah pernikahan yang sah.
content_ar: هذا الفصل من الكتاب يتناول موضوعات إسلامية مهمة في إطار منهج أهل السنة والجماعة.
